one_line_takeaway: A position paper proposing a plan/policy reference architecture for LLM agents and arguing that IPI defense needs security-aware dynamic replanning, tightly-constrained LLM-based security decisions over structured artifacts, and human-in-the-loop for irreducible ambiguity -- backed by argument and case studies, not experiments.
key_finding: As a position paper it asserts rather than measures. Central claims: (a) plan-execution isolation (as in CaMeL/dual-LLM) trades away utility because benign runtime changes and iterative tasks require replanning, and static task-agnostic policies produce both false positives (blocking a legitimate 'pay the bill from xyz@abc.com' whose recipient is in a shared artifact) and false negatives (a 'pay Joe back' task where a web-injected price inflates an unconstrained amount parameter); (b) the tension of 'LLM judges are themselves injectable' is resolvable by bounding the model's inputs to structured artifacts and its task to narrow adjudication, which also gives model-robustness research a more tractable target; (c) existing benchmarks create a false sense of security/utility because they lack dynamic multi-step tasks, dynamic attack objectives, and payload optimization -- illustrated with the cited statistic that the popular AgentDojo benchmark has only 6 of 97 tasks requiring policy update/replanning (this figure is attributed to ref [15] AgentDyn, NOT measured by these authors).
core_contribution: This is a POSITION paper (cs.CR) that argues for a system-level architecture and research agenda for defending general-purpose LLM agents against indirect prompt injection (IPI). Its own contributions are conceptual, not empirical: (1) a reference system architecture (Figure 1) built around six components -- Orchestrator, Plan/Policy Approver, Plan, Executor, Policy, and Policy Enforcer -- plus a formalization of 'plan' (an ordered sequence/DAG of execution steps e:=(a,I,O)) and 'policy' (a predicate pi(.) over steps/histories inducing a permitted-plan set). (2) Three normative positions: [P1] dynamic security-aware replanning AND dynamic policy updates are necessary for utility in realistic dynamic environments, so strict plan-execution isolation is too brittle; [P2] LLMs (or learned models) are essential for some context-dependent security decisions, but ONLY when the system strictly constrains what the model can observe and decide (feed it narrowly-scoped structured artifacts -- e.g. typed traces and plan/policy diffs -- not raw attacker-controlled text); [P3] some ambiguity (ambiguous language semantics like 'urgent', and ambiguous objective alignment like 'curl ... | bash' during bug repro) is unresolvable algorithmically and requires personalization / human-in-the-loop. (3) Two concrete design proposals under P2 -- decoupling instruction recognition from the instruction-following DECISION (verbalize candidate instructions, then adjudicate by provenance under three operating modes), and using an LLM to synthesize step-specific programmatic validators for environment feedback. (4) A critique of existing IPI benchmarks and an argument that system-level design is the 'skeleton' enabling system-model-human co-design.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against general-purpose, fully-autonomous LLM agents. The system and user prompts are TRUSTED; the environment is PARTIALLY COMPROMISED, i.e. malicious instructions are embedded in external/untrusted data segments (retrieved emails, web pages, third-party tool outputs) that hijack agent actions. Explicitly OUT of scope: orthogonal traditional techniques such as sandboxing (the paper focuses on threats fundamentally unsolvable by sandboxing), agents with predefined/limited-autonomy workflows, and direct prompt injection of the user/system prompt. The paper also stresses ADAPTIVE attackers who optimize payloads against a specific defense (e.g. RL-trained attacker models, genetic-style iterative refinement), which it argues current static benchmarks fail to capture.
limitations: By nature a vision/position paper: no implementation, no quantitative evaluation, and no evidence that the proposed architecture actually improves utility-security trade-offs in practice -- the failure scenarios are illustrative, not empirical. The authors concede using an AI model for security decisions PRECLUDES a 100% formal guarantee and reframe the goal as merely raising attacker cost / defeating low-effort attacks. Proposal 1 (verbalize-then-adjudicate) is acknowledged to face adaptive attacks that suppress verbalization, and relies on provenance tracing via word/substring matching that may be brittle. The paper explicitly declines to survey the field or cover background (defers to SoK refs [4],[11],[20]). Whether the structured-artifact bottleneck preserves enough information for utility is asserted, not demonstrated.

quantitative_results: AgentDojo has only 6 out of 97 tasks that require policy update/replanning (cited from ref [15] AgentDyn; a critique of an external benchmark, not an author-run measurement)
No author-produced quantitative results: this is a position paper with no experiments, tables, or measured metrics
baselines_compared: CaMeL [6] -- plan-execution isolation with a fixed input-only plan; characterized as removing the feedback/update loop, limiting dynamic tasks (discussed, not benchmarked)
Progent [17] -- programmable privilege control whose plan/policy update feeds unrestricted environment text to an LLM, so its update path is not security-aware
PFI [13] -- redacts tool responses / replaces with placeholders before the executor, reducing untrusted influence but hurting utility
AgentArmor [21] -- IFC-based data/control-flow tracking, but performed by an LLM judge over raw environment text, exposing it to adaptive attacks
DRIFT [14] -- user-query-based plan with an LLM judge deciding plan updates and another LLM removing malicious text; still steerable by adaptive attackers

AgentDojo [7], AgentDyn [15], InjecAgent [25], ASB [26] -- referenced as benchmarks the paper critiques for lacking dynamic tasks/objectives and payload optimization (analysis, not experimental comparison)
